title: Using Haber for Past Tense
description: Learn to use 'haber' to form past tense and practice object pronouns.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: We were learning how to use the present to talk about the past with 'haber'. For example, 'I have gone', 'I have eaten'. What do you think 'terminar' means?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Finish. 'Terminar' is to finish. Do you remember how to say 'I have' from 'haber'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: It's 'he'. Good. How would you say 'I have finished'? How do you make 'finished' from 'terminar'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. 'He terminado'. And how would you say 'I have finished it'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Lo he terminado. Good. Remember, object pronouns like 'lo', 'la', 'me', 'te' go before the conjugated verb. How would you say 'I have finished them'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Los he terminado. Good. For -ar verbs, we use -ado. For -er/-ir verbs, we use -ido. For example, 'vender' becomes 'vendido'.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How do you say 'I have lost'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: He perdido. Good. If you want to emphasize, you can say 'yo he perdido'. How do you say 'we have lost'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hemos perdido. Good. 'Nosotros' is for a male or mixed group, 'nosotras' for females. How would you say 'we have lost', emphasizing?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. 'Nosotros hemos perdido' or 'nosotras hemos perdido'. Now, how do you say 'he has lost'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ha perdido. To clarify it's 'he', you can say 'él ha perdido'. How would you say 'she has lost'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ella ha perdido. Good. Now, how do you say 'you have lost' informally?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Has perdido. To emphasize, say 'tú has perdido'. Now, let's practice object pronouns. How do you say 'I see it'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Lo veo. Good. How would you say 'I see her' or 'I see it' (feminine)?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: La veo. And 'I see them' (feminine)?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Las veo. Good. For masculine or mixed, 'los veo'. Now, how do you say 'he sees'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Él ve. And 'she sees'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ella ve. Good. How do you say 'we see'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Nosotros vemos or nosotras vemos. Good. 'Usted' is the formal 'you'. How would you say 'you see' formally?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Usted ve. Good. How would you say 'you see it' formally?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Lo ve usted or usted lo ve. Both are correct. How would you say 'they see'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ellos ven or ellas ven. Good. How would you say 'they see them'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ellos los ven or ellas las ven. Good. Remember, we have two sets of important words: subject pronouns like 'yo', 'él', 'ella', 'nosotros', and object pronouns like 'me', 'te', 'lo', 'la'.

outro.text: You've learned how to use 'haber' to form the past tense and practiced using object pronouns.
Try forming sentences with other verbs you know, like 'comer' or 'vivir', using 'haber'.
Remember, practice makes perfect. The more you use these structures, the more natural they will become.
